text.title: nosql-patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for nosql-patterns as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Choosing MongoDB "because microservices" without documenting access patterns first.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Postgres JSONB covers 80% of flexible-schema use cases; premature NoSQL adoption locks into a model that may not match future query needs.

failure-mode.description: Embedding arrays with unbounded growth in MongoDB (e.g., appending every event to a user document).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Documents grow past the 16MB limit; large documents degrade working set, increase index size, and slow full-document updates.

failure-mode.description: Missing indexes on MongoDB fields used in query predicates.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Unindexed queries cause collection scans; at scale this degrades from milliseconds to minutes.

failure-mode.description: Choosing a Cassandra/DynamoDB partition key without modeling the primary access pattern.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Partition key is immutable at scale; a wrong choice requires a full table copy to fix, causing downtime.

failure-mode.description: Storing Redis keys without TTL or eviction policy.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Keys accumulate until maxmemory is hit; Redis then either evicts randomly or rejects writes, causing application errors.

failure-mode.description: Agent uses raw keys in Redis without namespace prefix (user:123 vs {service}:user:123).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Multiple services sharing one Redis instance collide on identical key names, causing cross-service data corruption.
